sentences.text: Well, we went our business the same way.
Really kind of-- that-- that-- from that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was not in '42.
It was already-- because in '44, they took us away and that was kind of like-- not '40-- that winter, but I don't know if it was the end of-- that's '43, let's put it that way.
I made the mistake, the '42.
That was at the end of '43 or the beginning of '44 because then when it was already kind of before-- close to the-- the-- before they took us a way.
That's what it was.
Taken away.
Well, as I say, that-- when it came already before Pesach, we were kind of-- as I say, we knew from-- from the sheriff that they're talking about that they're gonna take away the Jews.
And then there is something else what we know, especially our family.
See, I am jumping from one to another.
I had two sisters living in Holland, and we got-- they were home before the war broke out.
It '35 or in '37 when they start already brewing the war in Germany and [? thing. ?] They came home for a vacation, and they didn't finish it.
They-- you know, they heard that thing.
They got a letter because just the sister came home, and my brother-in-law didn't.
And so they left, they went back.
And so after a while, we stop getting letters from them, from Holland.
And so we stopped.
We knew, well, it's a war and things.

By then, all of the sudden we got, I don't know which year, it was already the-- Hungarian time or whatever-- I really don't know-- we got a letter from them that, well, it was a card, that we could send a package.
There it was written out what it could be, in the [? food ?] family, something like that.
We could send a package.
That's all, but they didn't send nothing.
It was their name, but where?
I mean, it was an address there, but we don't know where that was.
In that time, I think they were already away from Holland in the concentration camp.
Do you know which one?
When I came-- the-- well, I'd have to jump again to thing.
When I came the second time to Auschwitz, and as we were in the train, that time was kind of open, the train, not like the first time.
And they were working men and-- after-- on the tracks and the other one unloading some stuff.
And actually, some-- everybody start hollering, from where are you?
From where are you?
And if somebody said, I'm from there, do you know who, do you know this?
One said, I'm from [?
Mukacevo. ?] I said I had a sister living in [?
Mukacevo ?] and her name is so and so but she is from Holland.
Oh, yeah, [?
Grimberger ?], yeah.
He said, yes, your sister was here.
I don't know where the brother is.
Well, that means that my sister was there.
She didn't have any children.
She was childless, but I don't know-- my brother-in-law's sister, she lives in Israel.
She is involved too somewhere in the museum and the things and the Jewish stuff.
So that's how I know that they were there, because he told me.
Emil, ?] you know, and [?
Frida. ?]
Like before they took us away?
Well, is-- it was-- Everybody was busy with the Pesach.
You know, we baked our own matzos, everybody.
The whole village got, you know, everything.
And-- in my sister's house [INAUDIBLE].
She was not banking herself, but there was already kind of equipped for baking matzos and everybody helped out.
And the whole process goes on with that.
And when the Pesach was over, it was brewing between us, the Jewish people, and the sheriff.
He said that they are talking, that the Jewish people are-- he doesn't know where or what, maybe he knew he just could not exactly say it, but he said it's-- get prepared.
Well, what could you get prepared if you don't know what's what?
You think, you know, what's-- you know, what.
What can you-- you know.
But then Pesach was over, and it was-- the end of Pesach was Shabbos.
And Sunday morning, it was the 20th or the 21st of April.
And usually didn't have the [INAUDIBLE] yet in the house, because we didn't bake.
And it was very hard to get that.
Well, we had out own flour from the things.
That-- there was a mill and we-- it was done.
So we had-- but-- we didn't bake.
But it always-- was always like, after Pesach, that day, we went to [?
Polyana. ?] To-- there was a bakery to take-- bring bread, till we bake our own.
And the bread-- the bread was going on little carts in the [INAUDIBLE], not just for the Jewish people, for everybody.
So, naturally, the youngsters get together.
And my sister's boy, he was about 11 or 12 years old, the oldest.
And other kind of 13-, 14-, 15-year-old boys kind of got together from the family and everybody got like, take my carts too and bring for me a pound or two or whatever bread.
But when they came-- it's just kind of like about two, three kilometers before that village, before [?
Polyana. ?] I had a brother living there.
I have the picture there from their house.
And when they came, there was a bridge with a river and the bridge to go to, and there was already the gendarme.
They didn't let in already in that thing because they were over there.
And-- Barry was living there, my husband, in that direction, after that village, after [?
Polyana, ?] so-- like a couple kilometers after that.
And they were already rounding up there the Jews.
So they came back and-- and said that they are not letting in in there.
And so my father-- maybe my father knew more from the sheriff, just didn't told us-- because I remember I was-- it was, you know, right away after Pesach.
The spring is coming and in the backyard there was-- through the winter and the snow or whatever it was, an old-- those big plums tree fell down so we went to clean up the backyard for the summer.
There were other of all kinds, apple trees and things.
And as my father was holding on one side the big log from that tree to bring it out down from-- there was a little hill, and I was in the other side.
And he came around to hold my sister's boy-- boy and said they won't let us in there.
We won't have any bread today.
You know, children, they did not know.
They just were worried that they won't have bread, still we were not gonna bake.
And my father said to me then-- like-- he said-- he called me by the Jewish name, [INAUDIBLE]-- put it down, we won't need it anymore.
That's what he said to me.
And we went in, and I don't even remember what happened to [INAUDIBLE].
It was like this.
To this day, I feel-- I went in kind of like-- they call it-- like-- a lump in the stomach, kind of like just press thing.
Would you believe it, it never went away, even now.
All right, I am happy, when the happy times are, you know, it's OK.
I don't say that I am always, that I'm-- you know, because life goes on.
I'm happy.
I think about any little thing, any little thought of home, any little thought of whatever, it's there.
It never went away.
So when that-- everybody start already getting prepared, [INAUDIBLE] the first thing is knapsacks.

Everybody had suitcases, and we had our suitcases anyway but that's not-- you know, everybody I could take-- I don't remember how it was, if the sheriff came to say or what, I really don't, but us Jews, between ourselves, everybody start running to each other and giving ideas what we gonna do, what to do.
Well, the first thing, we didn't know when exactly they gonna come to our village so we-- and as I said, we had the-- the chickens, you know, and the [INAUDIBLE] was in the other side.
Not like [?
Polyana ?] was up towards one side and [INAUDIBLE] was in the other side, there was the [INAUDIBLE].
So we had to go there somehow, but till the-- you know, till the-- in the daytime we could go with the-- with the yellow stars.
But we had shortcuts anyway to go through forest stuff to-- to the fields.
So the boys went, and everybody from every household was and two, three chickens and women start cooking.
And nobody ate, because there was no appetite to eat.
And we didn't know-- it was no refrigerators like to put it away, it came-- started to get warm.
The next day, again, you know, the third day it was thrown out and start anew, start baking cookies, like non-perishable, something hard stuff.
We start sewing knapsacks.
We should have more on our backs, instead-- not instead of suitcases but besides suitcases.
And-- and it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was an awful week.
I was 26, I think.
I would have been 20-- yeah.
Something like that, yeah-- 25, 26.
No, no.
So that was going on a whole week like that, that preparation.
And we start doing through the night.
We had a neighbor there right beside us.
The sheriff lived across that-- that creek, but right over the fence, we had a neighbor.
And at night, we went and we took over.
We had a lot of sacks of rye and corn beddings.
We had a lot of materials for-- it's different kind of bedding, like big pillows and feathers, you know, with-- from the geese because we had our own geese.
And whatever we could, my brother when he came home from Prague, he had beautiful suits.
We gave the suits.
As a matter of fact, when I came back, one suit she gave us back.
We got-- Barry got married in it.
And we gave some porcelain, some silverware, and she was very good when I came home.
And I came home-- at that time, when I was home, I got from her every week a baked bread.
She gave me somethings were-- were going.
And he was wearing-- the-- the-- the grain was [? growing. ?] And she gave me back some silver things, and we had a [INAUDIBLE] set of beautiful porcelain.
She gave me--
You remember her name?
Bochkov.
Anna Bochkov.
Yeah.
And so we start to liquidate things like that.
And in-- in the meantime I don't remember if the cows were before or that week to take the cows there.
I don't remember, for to give it over for the government.
And that was going around after a whole week of preparation.
And on Friday, we saw the gendarmes in the village.
There was quite a few of them.
And actually, the sheriff had to go with them, and the-- the-- the crier came.
It was just-- like-- we were living in our house, was kind of like in the-- almost middle of the village.
And-- and it was announced that so and so, the-- the Jewish people should take whatever they can with them, and they should meet in the school.
At the end of the village was the school.
Hungarians, Hungarians with the feathers.
Some of them before, they were there since '39.
They were-- some of them were very, very nice, very nice.
As a matter of fact, now I remember that we knew quite in advance that you'll be taken away, because I had been by the creek in the front of our yard there.
Well, maybe I looked a little bit better than now, and the-- and the sheriff was living right across from that thing.
And I was there by the-- by the creek there.
It's like our yard, and it's connected right away.
And he came over to talk to me.
And I asked him, when the time is gonna come, are you going to come after me?
Just think.
He made kind of like a smile, how do I say that in English?
A grin?
A grin.
Yeah, kind of like a friendly-- friendly grin, not to really kind of friendly grin, but he was not there.
There were other ones.
And everybody went there-- there to the village.
And there we were looked over for valuables.
Some people put-- you know, we took along the jars of preserves, from-- you know, like jams.
Some people throw in some of the gold and the things.
Like for instance, a married woman did not take off the wedding ring, because they knew that they have to have a wedding ring, you know.
And they broke the jars.
They were looking places which kind of could be [INAUDIBLE].
Well, whoever had really valuable like that, jewelry, it was hidden in the ground at home before.
That was a story about in our-- my place.
This is one of them that I found after I came home.
[INAUDIBLE].
And there were bigger ones.
My sister's, [?
Frida's ?] and her husband-- well her husband was not-- that's quite a different story, not for here.
You know.
Hiding, like what?
No, no, that was kind of that time when the sheriff was telling us that we could-- you know, if time is going to come.
He just didn't know exactly about the thing, but that's what it was, nothing else.
There was no place, kind of, you see.

It's-- [? things. ?] And so when we came there to the school, and it was Friday afternoon and then everybody got back, and we, oh, got a big pot with soup and chicken in it to carry with one side and the other side, and we left it there by the school because everything has to go in a hurry.
In a hurry, they start to put us already at that time like five in a row.
Well, you couldn't do that there, because that was a stony place.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so but we came down.
It took about five minutes to come down to the main road, and then there on the main road they-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] so we went and walked to [INAUDIBLE].
In [INAUDIBLE], they put us in the shul.
Like an hour and a half to go.
We all walked.
Men, women, and children with the gendarmes by the side.
Everybody behaved and everybody-- people were crying.
Going why, what's going on, where are we going?
They don't-- I don't know if somebody dared to ask the gendarme.
I-- I really don't know but whoever I seen in the front, you know, for me, I could see them now there in the road.
It was nice, it was not rainy.
No.
It was a nice weather.
And we came to [INAUDIBLE], and they put us in the shul.
And over there was already from another village in the shul.
Other people from Barry's, from my husband's village, they were already somewhere.
I don't know where they were, probably already in the-- in the-- in the ghetto, in [INAUDIBLE], because there were two ghettos.
And-- and we slept there in the shul, on the floor, on the chairs sitting, by the [INAUDIBLE], by the whatever.
I cried there too when one from our village, he didn't came home.
And--
From my family was my parents and me and my sister with four children.
The husband was already in that-- those working.
I never could say it, forced labor or labor force.

And another sister with two children without the husband, and there was a sister-in-law, which my brother was already away too, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from [INAUDIBLE], from that next where we pass by, that village where one of my brothers were living with two children.
We find him already there in the shul.
He was in the morning taken away or what.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, sister-- two sisters and a sister-in-law were there, and then my sister-in-law had a mother there and-- and my sisters.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No other sisters.
I don't remember if was there-- yeah, [?
Shia. ?] Yeah, [?
Shia ?] and his brother with a wife.
That's my-- an uncle from my niece's which they-- so that's how many we were there.
And from [?
Polyana, ?] that brother that they did not let through to the bakery, he was-- he was in the other.
It was like in the same city but another there.
There was brick factories.
So we came to [INAUDIBLE], we slept over there.
And on Shabbas, they put us on a civilian train because there was no-- so on a civilian train, we went to Mukacevo.
A regular train.
With seats, a regular train.
I don't remember.
I really don't--
A lot of people.
A lot of people because from villages-- from small villages from around, from around.
So--
No, no.
So we-- we went to Mukacevo.
We came-- I don't remember, it wasn't at night because as I say, it's just an hour from there.
I don't remember how the train was going slow or fast or whatever, but we came there.
And we were-- they settled us in the-- in the things.
There were no walls on-- on the thing.
It was just a roof.
It was a long one.
It's almost like-- you know, like a bowling alley when there's-- it's no walls but just a long thing.
There it start-- there it start.
In-- in the ghetto that you lose the humanity, the humiliation.
Like, as they say, there was kind of-- it was like-- it was probably maybe a little bit wider because this how much it was the walk.
And it was a little bit wider as we could sleep, you know.
So like, we were-- our family was sleeping here, one of my sisters with the children, my parents and me.
My girlfriend, [INAUDIBLE], she was right here because her family started so we wanted to sleep together.
So-- and want to sleep there.
A little bit further was sleeping another family.
Right here across from us was my brother from [INAUDIBLE], from the other thing, with the two children, and my two nieces with their mother.
You know, they were four children like on the other side, kind of.
Everybody tried like, to family together.
And if it was raining, depending on where the wind was blowing.
If it was raining, and the wind was blowing, the rain came in.
No, it was just a little bit left over bricks so we started kind of to put up like this high up kind of, you know, because it was scattered, the things of the next day he tried to do.
And the next day, they went-- came.
They-- all the men had to shave their beards down, my father too.
[INAUDIBLE].
He said there was no use of it to others.
And-- well, he was already a neighbor there, and they were talking and my father said, they say that [INAUDIBLE] everybody [INAUDIBLE].
Something-- I don't know from-- he mentioned where something he-- place he saw.
And-- but--
The Hungarians.
The Hungarians.
No.
No, the Hungarians, but they were under the Nazi--
Orders.
Orders, yeah.
Oh yeah, hundreds, hundreds of--
I don't know, I don't know.
No, it was not maybe really a thousand or maybe--
In the hundreds, yeah.
Maybe it was thousands.
I know hundreds, but, as I say, the first few days, you know, we had from home some stuff.
Once a day they gave us soup-- soup, you know, things.
So we had to stay in the line for it but then, you know, besides us, especially there were children, everybody tried, oh, I don't need it.
You know, if a mother said, Regina, have a [INAUDIBLE].
Oh no, I'm not hungry, no matter how because you were pushing it, stretching it because we didn't know what's what.
And so-- but it was really terrible there, because the-- the sanitation started getting bad outside.
The-- the thing started raining, shoes in the mud, kind of sinking in, and the latrine, it was not prepared.
There was just like an outhouse, you know, for two, three people but when there are tens going, you know, it was-- well I don't know how to say it-- kind of like being embarrassed.
You know.
People could imagine what it is.
It starts kind of like backing up.
And there was to wash, all right, we had like a dish or two from home [INAUDIBLE].
There was no water there.
There was water, you know, where they made bricks, they make from the ground because they bake them.
Make them first like those, I don't know, the adobes.
They build first, you know, that thing and then they bake them so they become--
